speaker: interviewer
text: Today is December 10th, 1995. This is an interview with Milton Schwartz. You were saying that on February 12th, 1944, the Russians had come into your area, and you decided to return to your hometown, Lutsk.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was actually when I found out that the farmer didn't tell me the whole truth, that only the partisan brigade took the town. The Germans got scared. They ran. But the army was not in yet. The army didn't come in yet. But still, the town was liberated from the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came home. I walked along the street. People see me. What? You're still alive? They're all shocked that I'm still alive. And lo and behold, I come up to my house, and I open the door. And who do I find? A porter who worked at the same brewery my father worked, grabbed our house. And he has the new title of the house. He's the owner of the house. And he grabs me. Before I had a chance to step into the house, he grabs my neck and starts choking me. Where's your father? Where's your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I knew he's not out to do good for me. He's going to kill me, because he inherited the house, and I'm a survivor. I'll take the house back, because after the Russians came in, every owner is entitled to his own house-- especially if it's registered to the owner.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said to him-- I didn't lose my head. Why is a fox foxy? Because he has to watch out for the wolf. I had to watch out for many wolves, for so many animals, that I said to him, my father is an officer of the partisans. Once I said the word 'partisans,' he stopped choking me, and he said, where is he? I said, he's coming. He went in to see a neighbor here. I told him I'm coming to see the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, come in. Come in the house. I'll give you something to eat. I said, oh, no. He's not out good for me. He'll poison me if I eat something in his house. And I went to some other house, where there was a Jewish man already, [INAUDIBLE] Berger, the man who took me there. He came back from the village, because his farmer told him too before for that, because he was kept by a farmer. And he came. He took over the house. The man who took the house, somebody gave it to him back. He said, here-- oh-- here the house. Here's the house. Here is the-- here are the keys. And we thank you. Here's the whole house. And I went to him. Lo and behold, at night time, the Germans made another counter attack, and we had to run for our lives. We had to run to another town, Rovne. In Rovne the Russian army already came in. And one night under way near the [NON-ENGLISH], near the park of the city where the capital house was from the capital of our-- of our town because it was [NON-ENGLISH], the capital of the town, I met my brother-in-law. And we went. Now we are together with my brother-in-law. He grabbed me, and he starts hugging me. And he told me the story about my sister, what happened. And then we started walking, going to Rovne. On the way, we saw the Russian army coming, the [NON-ENGLISH]. And the Russian army repelled back the German attack, and we end up in Rovne. In Rovne is another chapter.

speaker: interviewee
text: We are in Rovne. We go on out next day to look for food. A Russian soldier, like this, with a big gun, meets us in the street. Passport? [NON-ENGLISH] means paper, passport.

speaker: interviewee
text: We are survivors. We didn't have it. We came out of the woods. He didn't let us go. He took us to the jail, and he put us in jail. No harm. They didn't do us any harm. They gave me something to eat. They gave me my brother something to eat, but no, they didn't let us go free. Then a few-- two days later, they take us to the station. And we come to the station, to this railroad station. There's thousands of people that the Russians have caught, thousands of Ukrainians, [NON-ENGLISH], murderers, who yesterday killed Jews. And we are two Jews between a sea of murderers. Because when the Russian-- waged war, when the Germans attacked Russia, the Ukrainians shot at the Russian soldiers. They were very angry at the Russians.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Russians are very angry at Ukrainians. Now they want to catch him, and they want to send him back to the army, to the special brigades where they don't come out alive. And I and my brother-in-law between these peoples.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we are in a cattle train with 80 people, 90 people in it. They put a big stove inside for tea, [INAUDIBLE], hot water. And they gave us a piece of bread to eat, and they took us deep into Russia. In five days, in eight days we are in Bryansk in Russia, central Russian, about 500 kilometers from Moscow. And they want to make a soldier out of me.

speaker: interviewee
text: They asked me how old I am. I say, I'm only 16 years old. So 16, they say, why didn't you say that you were a young boy, that you were not 19 or 18? I said, because nobody asked me. And they took me to-- they start to send me to the army. They put a stamp on my passs-- they gave me a pass-- and they took off my lapels from the army [INAUDIBLE], because I didn't have any clothes. And they send me to Stalingrad to work in a factory where they're building tanks. Meanwhile, your brother-in-law was older?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother-in-law was older. He was taken into the army. But they asked him what kind of profession he is. He told them that I am a tailor. So a tailor, they didn't want to send to the-- in the-- to get killed on the front with a punishment on the Ukrainians. They took him special to a different brigade. And they made him to work for the generals, for their wives, to make coats. And he did not go in the-- in the front. He was standing there, and he was there already in Bryansk. I was in Stalingrad, and they sent me into Stalingrad to work. And I'll make a long story short what happened. In 1946, I joined a Polish organization, Polish organization. [NON-ENGLISH]. It means Organization of the Polish Patriot. Because even so the Russians forced on me the Russian citizenship, because I was considered a Pole because I was born in Poland. And I did not want to stay in Russia, in my hometown. I figured because I received letters from my hometown that Ukrainians are killing Jews, especially who want to take away the houses. For a dollar, for two, they'll kill them. And once he's killed, nobody is to ask for the house. The Russians are not going to take it away from them. They are going to give them as an inheritance.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I decided, my life, I worked very hard for it. I'm not going back to my hometown. I'm going back to Poland, where nobody is interested in my living, my life and so. The Russians let you just leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, once I made out papers, I went to the police. I got a paper that I'm a good citizen. I was in good order. I don't owe the government any money. And they let me go back to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went to Poland. I said, they even questioned me, why do you want to go to Poland? You were born in Lutsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I'm a Polish citizen. I was born in Poland. I want to go to Poland. My idea to Poland was to get to the American zone in Germany, and to get to America. Because this was my-- this was my whole dream to come to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to Poland. And in Poland, the same saga started. In Poland, there was a big-- a big pogrom in Kielce. There was a blood libel against the Jewish people, that they are using a child for blood for Passover. And they killed 47 Jewish people-- survivors with marks on their hand from the concentration camp. So I didn't want to stay in Poland either, because in Poland after the war they killed 1,600 Jews. In Germany, not a single Jew was killed. In Germany, the country was soaked in blood. After they lost the war, the Germans are a very, very disciplined people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now you could kill. Now you can't. So this time, the same killers who yesterday killed people become very decent, fine citizens, bitte schoen, danke schoen, guten morgen. And this is the way they are. They are very disciplined people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went from Poland. I sneaked to Czechoslovakia. From Czechoslovakia, I went to Vienna. From Vienna, I went to-- to Germany because it was a free-- for the-- for the people who survived-- Jewish people who survived the war-- I had-- I looked for friend. I look for relatives. So every country looked at you sympathetically. You look for [? relatives? ?] OK, go. Go. You could go. You could travel. As a matter of fact, they didn't have to pay for the training.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I came to American zone. Once I came to American zone, I sent away a letter to America, which I send away a letter [? with ?] Stalingrad. And this is chapter 2 that I want to touch. In Stalingrad, I walk the street, and I see a boy who's sitting there, a boy, Avrom. His name was Avrom. I forgot his second name. And a good-looking boy. He worked for a house of women. You can imagine what a good-looking boy will do with Russian women in a house of women. He was taking care of their safety there. And they were going to work at the factory. And I take a look. I see a big envelope. And under the envelope in the corner is a big airplane, a red airplane. The [INAUDIBLE] was written down, USA.

speaker: interviewee
text: That airplane USA, I said to him, are you Amhu? Amhu was the password. Are you Jew? He says, yes. I'm Jewish. I said where are you from? I am from Krakow. Famous town, Krakow, where is, Krakow. I said, what are you-- where are you getting-- getting letters from America? He says, yeah. I get letters from my grandfather. He could, because he came from Poland, from-- from Krakow. He had the address he had from the home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wartime, Russia and America was allies. Here I sent a letter to Brooklyn. And he got himself the address, and he's writing to his grandfather. Little did I realize that America is so easy to get an address that, like this, you pick up a telephone, you call. My family's name, my mother's name is [INAUDIBLE] Nobody-- any [INAUDIBLE] is my relative. Any [INAUDIBLE] in the book-- there's very few-- is my relatives.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said to him, could you write to your grandfather? I know my uncle lives in Brooklyn. I said Brooklyn a little town. Because maybe you'll find my uncle. His name is Sam [INAUDIBLE] and I have a tante, Rose Lindeman. But Lindeman didn't have a telephone. [INAUDIBLE] had a telephone. So lo and behold, he says to me, give me 300 rubles. 300 rubles is two months'-- two months' labor. I made 150 rubles a month, working very hard as a fireman to fire the fires of the tanks, to meld the steel. I worked like a son of a gun then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, he needed the money to support his habit. You know, I don't want to belittle. You know, let him rest in peace where he is, or be wherever he is. And lo and behold, I sit down and write 16 pages, written tears and blood, telling what has happened to my family, what happened the way they made them go down, the way they made the women to undress, they way they beat them. And my grandparents was their father and mother. My father-- my grandparents was their father and mother. My mother and my sister made everybody. I send away a letter. Three months later, the letter comes back. The letter was red stamped. The letter was not accepted. His letter is gone. My letter don't go to America. I didn't say nothing against the Russians. I just said good things about the Russian army. The Russian army saved my life.

speaker: interviewee
text: If it wouldn't be for the Russians, who knows? My bones would have been dried already. The Russians-- and I didn't say nothing against the Russians. The letter came back. I came to this fellow again. I don't want to-- [? I don't know where ?] to use it, not nice news, but what-- I go to him. I said tell me, how come your letter go to America? And what's my letter? Why  doesn't it go? I paid you 300 rubles.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, give me 150 more rubles. I'll tell you how you should write a letter that should go to America. And I am so broke now that I say, look. I didn't have any money. I still owe you. I'll pay you slowly because I don't-- I don't have enough money. Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, look. It's simple. They finish the letter. Take out  the envelope. Take another envelope in stamps and write down in Russian. [SPEAKING RUSSIAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: Long live-- long live Stalin. Long live the Russian army. Long live the Russian people. Then the letter will go. This letter went to America. But this letter did a lot of damage to me with what I had said.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? Because when I came to America and 1949, the 3rd of March. And I'm sitting already in my uncle's house, and we're talking. We're talking about the whole [NON-ENGLISH], about the family. I asked him uncle, what happened to my letter? I sent you a 16-page letter. When I sent the letter, it was in 1944 when the Germans burned 20,000 people a day in Auschwitz and the other towns. We were liberated because we were near the Russian border. We were liberated before. Why-- what did you do? Did you publish the letter in the Jewish papers, in the American papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, let me tell you what happened. I took the letter to the forwards, and the forwards man told me. And they're sitting there, go on, speak to the boss. And I went in to speak to the boss of the publisher. And the publisher sat down white like the wall.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my god. It's true whatever they're saying, they're killing the Jewish people. And I said, well, what has happened? Did you publish the letter? He says, listen, [NON-ENGLISH], Mr. Jew. He says to him, there's an order from the [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] in English means the upper-ups, because I told them in Jewish. There's an order of upper-ups not to publish any atrocities committed against the Jews. So my uncle is there perplexed, [NON-ENGLISH], why? Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Apparently, this is what the publisher, told him, my uncle told me. Before he died, people told me that I tell you that it breaks my heart. He says  to him, apparently, the American army will say they are fighting a war to save the Jew. So they want to know what I publish. When I did this saying on a memorial in the Hebrew Institute in Riverdale, Rabbi Avi Weiss [INAUDIBLE], and there was big dignitaries. Between the big dignitaries were Senator Moynihan. After I finished, I came over, and I said it with heart and feelings. After I came down, there were about 3,000, 4,000 people, people who vote for democratic, whatever. And I came down from the [NON-ENGLISH]. Moynihan came over to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: He took me around. He's a very, very heavy-set fellow. He took me around. He said, Mr Schwartz, what you just said to the people here, it is true what you just said? It happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, Mr. Moynihan, Senator, it happened to me. I am a witness to it. But my uncle told me. I sent a letter in 1944 when 20,000 Jewish people a day was burned in Auschwitz. But Majdanek, in other town-- another in Treblinka, in Sobibor, and other-- and other places, was no-- nobody would lift a finger to help. As a matter of fact, I said, when Roosevelt mentioned one word, they're going to pay for it, they stopped killing the  Jews from Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: He takes out a little booklet. A little booklet. What's your name? What's-- what's your address? What's your telephone number? Would you mind? I even pay you for your transportation. I swear to you the way it was. He could deny if he want me to right to my face.

speaker: interviewee
text: And let me know. I'll call you. And you'll come to Washington DC. You'll testify that during the cover up, during the Second World War, there was a cover up. He's still calling. I'm still waiting for his call. So this is a chapter by itself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just ask you a question going back one minute. Wasn't there an incident in the summer of '41 where you were beaten by a German soldier?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because there's so many incidents I could tell.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, I just thought we should mention that.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is not so more-- this is not important. The beatings, so what? We are-- we are accustomed to beatings. At least I'm alive. But this is what I want to tell you what has happened to my own sister with a lot of women and children. And that's what I want to bring in. It will probably be painful to a lot of people to listen. But please understand, I'm not trying to put a finger on anybody else. I'm trying to say, the murderers, people should know who is the murderer, and people should know who is the victim. And I tell you what I saw with my own eyes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the [INAUDIBLE] was-- before the ghetto was formed, a big truck came with a big trailer truck, like I see on the highways. Looks like a house, with blinds I never saw in my life before, because we use cotton [? foldings, ?] what do you call it? Covers in the windows. Blinds. This is something special, America.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we saw this truck. And the truck had red, big red crosses. And the first time in my life as a child, I saw German fraulein, blonde with red crosses hats on their heads, red crosses armbands here. And they bend down from the truck. The truck stood there on the streets, and they asked for people who did not have any milk for their children, when the children are starving from hunger, they should go in the truck. They'll take them to the village. They'll feed them. They'll give them milk to drink. And they feed them, and they brink them back. Needless to say, did we ever thought-- I didn't even know from the beginning that this has happened, that the Germans are capable of making gas chambers on wheels, which now came out. There are many gas chambers. They did it to many towns, to many cities. The name of the-- of the cross, they put [INAUDIBLE] the Jewish people. Women were standing, fighting to bring their children in. My sister couldn't make it. She was a strong woman, but she had a little girl on the floor under standing here, a little girl on their arm. You know, [NON-ENGLISH]. And she couldn't get into the truck, because all the other people kept on pushing. And needless to say, not a single woman, not a single child, came back. We find out later and after the war from the farmers in the [NON-ENGLISH], in the area where they kept on doing the killing, that there they dumped them in open graves, the children with them, and their mothers. This what I want to bring. This is very, very important to bring up.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw this--

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw it with my own eyes. I saw the truck. I saw the truck. I saw the [INAUDIBLE]. I saw the red crosses. Who didn't know? Red cross, you see [INAUDIBLE] a salvation. The women are getting milk. Who wouldn't take to get milk for their children when they didn't have milk for so many months, weeks and weeks. Before-- don't forget, from the 24th, 25th of June, till the ghetto, when the ghetto started, there was a lot of incidents.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is only one incident that I could see. What about the other thousands and thousands and millions incidents that other people who are not-- who are dead who can't tell the tales? There are so many that it's unbelievable. The Germans wouldn't dare to do it in Germany. That was against the Jewish people, what they did in our areas. And there were nine survivors, you said, from Lutsk.

speaker: interviewee
text: From this area that I came out and I survived. Naturally after-- after a few more weeks later, [INAUDIBLE] people came back from the army. As a matter of fact, you see the pictures. You see a lot of people stay with the army uniforms. And other people were hidden someplace in other villages. But in this area that I was, we came back with nine people that I count was alive. Maybe later on, more, a couple more people came in. But you could count them by the fingers. Like I was there now in 1989, I was in my hometown simply when my brother-in-law came to Russia. He told the a real hell of a story that when he came to say Kaddish-- meaning the prayer for the dead on their grave site for his wife and children-- my sister-- he finds skeletons and little bones of babies thrown about all around.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to look around. And he find a pair of pants lying there-- painter's pants. He took a stick to lift the pants. Golden teeth fell out. So he didn't have to say, one and one, what is happening? He say, oh, yeah, they're hanging up the skeletons on the-- on the trees. They marked the dead people. They-- they're digging the graves [INAUDIBLE]. Because in Europe, if you lose a tooth, you don't have any plastic. They put gold. Even the poor man collects a few dollars for the golden tooth. So they kept on digging, digging, digging the grave site in order to dig out the skeletons to pull the teeth out. So we were so incensed-- so incensed of this, that this is happening after so many years, atrocity happening against the Jewish people, that we decided to go and visit Russia, and plead with them. They should make a national shrine. Close the place up.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was one woman who was hung because she was a partisan. They put a big monument of marble. And they spent about a million to beautify the place, one person. Here is 28,000 people died, and nobody thinks about protecting the area and putting a monument on it. It's only a little stone, like I showed you in the pictures, about the height of my shoulders.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I come into this Russian commissar. It was still Russia. 1989 was Russia still intact. Russian commissar sits there. There is Stalin. There is Lenin on the wall. There are all the dignitaries around. And I-- and they gives us coffee to drink. And I rip out my American passport out of my pocket. And I say, look, here is an American passport, not written that I am a Jew. You have on my, was in  the last passport, written [NON-ENGLISH] Jew. [NON-ENGLISH] he's a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you have your passport that a Jew is a Jew while he's alive. When he's dead, he's no more a Jew. He's a Russian citizen. Why don't you put up a monument, and put up a border, a fence around. Youll pay for the fance. We'll pay the price of the fence. You pay for the monument. American organization from Lutsk will pay it. They are authorized to say it. And we'll do that. Please, stop these atrocities. He said, no, no. Our Ukranians are not digging. The [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] means the Gypsies are digging the graves. I said, we know different. We know that your man come here after church, the Ukrainians come here after church and dig the graves in order to pull the golden teeth. And teeth, gold is a lot of money for them. The Russian commissar was very receptive. As a matter of fact, I even put my yarmulke on, but he gave me [INAUDIBLE]. He was very receptive, and he said to me, we'll do, only one thing we want from you. We'll pay for the money. We'll pay for the monument. You pay for the fence.

speaker: interviewee
text: You just tell your-- your citizens, your people, Lutsk and [INAUDIBLE] from the other areas, they should come visit us. Why? Because they charge us a hundred dollars a day. [LAUGHTER] A hundred dollars a day for staying there. They figured, look at the money that could be gotten.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, this is what happened. Lo and behold, a year later my friends went. I didn't go, because I-- I was broken hearted, secondly. And I couldn't afford it to go a second time. They went a second time. They made a monument of marble. I put it someplace, a big picture. I can't find it. And they wrote down this time, here are buried so and so many thousand Jewish people, the Jewish religion, and in Russian and in English and in Ukrainian and Jewish. And in Hebrew. There was a big delegation from Israel with the Israeli flags. Before, if you have an Israeli flag, they'll put you into jail for 20 years, for 10 years. Now this time, they allowed Israeli flags. They made a big ceremony. They were talking like they were part of the helpers. Which was they were the murderers, a lot of Ukrainians. But they say, look. We want you to come back. We want to build Ukraine. Anyway, this is what happened. Hopefully maybe this holy land, holy ground that this cemetery, the Jewish people is going to stay intact. They're not going to disturb the bones or the skeletons of the people-- hopefully.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's ask a little bit about when you got to Germany, you stayed in a displaced persons' camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, stayed in a displaced persons camp, and I worked for the American armies as a electrician.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you contacted your uncle and aunt?

speaker: interviewee
text: I contacted my relatives. They sent me papers. And-- and during the time, I made a big mistake. I was born in Poland. But I could have said I was born in Russia, because it was Russian land. If I would have said, had the foresight, to say I was born in Russia, I would have come to America right away. But I said, I was born in Poland. Poland had a very small quarter. I had to wait for three years. In 1949, the 3rd of March, I came to America. My hands opened up the house for me. They opened up-- and I came Thursday. Monday I got a job. I got a job on my own. Very little pay, but I got a job. I got a job in Brooklyn. I passed by, I see the Jewish Hospital of Brooklyn. I went in. Sure enough, I spoke to the lady. She spoke to me in German. And she got me a job-- a electrician. $1,800 Dollars a year, six days a week. I put it down on the calculator. I put it on a calculator. It was $0.27 an hour. But I was very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went back to school, and you got your degree.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to school. I got my degree. I went to school. I worked really hard. In daytime, I worked. And at nighttime, I went to school to City College. The street, needless to say, the streets were more safe than now. I'm sorry. But I got a degree. I got a degree as an electrical-- stationary electrical engineer. So I-- I worked-- I got a job here. And I worked in this place for so many years, for 46 years in the Bronx. And I am very, very-- and my title is, I'm the Director of Engineering now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you got married in--

speaker: interviewee
text: I got married in 1953. I married an American girl born on the east side. And we have now four children. And we have, God bless them, nine-- nine grandchildren. Our oldest granddaughter [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Our oldest granddaughter is-- is 20 years old. [DRINKS] She wants to get married. So hopefully we'll have a simcha.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you felt it was very important for you to tell this story because of all the denial of the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this was the most important thing that happened to me, to-- to not to be silent. Because I find out that if I am silent, and everybody of all of us is survivors, we are going on in age. We are not going to stay young forever. And denying while we-- we are still alive, the denying this has ever happened. The deny that my parents, my brothers, and little David, my brother David, they were all hoaxes. They never existed. For this reason, I decided that I'll do everything in my power to tell and to speak and to say what has happened to our people, individually. In the [INAUDIBLE], the people who were killed, they have stories, worse stories than I had to tell. But the live-- the people who are alive should at least, they are obligated, their parents, their brothers and sisters, to their own people, not to keep silent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.
